section.title: Vector spaces

introduction.p: Earlier, we considered \mathbb{R}^n, the set of n-dimensional column vectors. We now introduce a more general concept of “vector”, as an element of an abstract vector space. Basically, vectors are entities that can be added and scaled. While some vectors look like lists of numbers (for example, column vectors, row vectors), other kinds of vectors don’t look like lists of numbers at all (for example, functions, polynomials). Part of the power of linear algebra comes from our ability to find vector spaces in many unexpected places.

Much of the content of this chapter will be a repetition of things we have already seen in the context of \mathbb{R}^n. For example, we will be talking about linear combinations, linear independence, spanning sets, bases, subspaces, linear transformations, and so on. We initially introduced these concepts in the context of the vector space \mathbb{R}^n, so that they would be easier to understand. We will now see that they in fact apply to all vector spaces.

In many courses, vectors first appear as arrows in the plane or as lists of numbers in \mathbb{R}^n. In this section, we will step back and ask a more basic question: what properties make a collection of objects behave like vectors?

In this activity, you will examine several different sets together with rules for addition and scalar multiplication. Your goal is not to check a list of axioms, but to identify which structural features seem essential and which failures cause problems.

statement.p: Consider the following sets, each equipped with the usual addition and scalar multiplication.

ol.li: The set \mathbb{R}^2 of ordered pairs (x,y).
The set of all polynomials of degree at most 2.
The set of all real-valued functions defined on \mathbb{R}.

statement.p: For each set:

ol.li: Give an example of adding two elements.
Give an example of multiplying an element by a scalar.
Identify one feature that all three sets seem to have in common.

statement.p: Now consider the following collections. In each case, the objects look familiar, but the set has been restricted in some way.

ol.li: The set of all polynomials with constant term equal to 1.
The set of all vectors in \mathbb{R}^2 whose coordinates are positive.
The set of all functions f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} such that f(0)=0.

statement.p: For each example:

ol.li: Decide whether the set still behaves like a space of vectors.
Find a specific operation (addition or scalar multiplication) that causes a problem, if one exists.
Explain why this failure is significant.

statement.p: In \mathbb{R}^2, there is a special vector that satisfies v + \mathbf{0} = v for every vector v.
For each of the following sets, discuss whether such a special element exists.

ol.li: Polynomials of degree at most 2.
Polynomials with constant term equal to 1.
Functions that satisfy f(0)=0.

statement.p: What role does this special element seem to play in determining whether a set should be considered a vector space?

conclusion.p: The examples in this activity suggest that “being a vector” is not about what objects look like, but about how they behave under addition and scalar multiplication. In the next section, these ideas will be distilled into a precise definition of a vector space.

subsection.title: Definition of vector spaces

definition.title: Vector space
definition.p: Let K be a field. A vector space over K is a set V equipped with two operations of addition and scalar multiplication, such that the following properties hold:

ul.li: A3. The existence of an additive unit: there exists an element \zerovec \in V such that for all \uvec, \uvec + \zerovec = \uvec.
A4. The law of additive inverses: \uvec + (-\uvec) = \zerovec.
SM1. The distributive law over vector addition: k(\uvec + \vvec) = k\uvec + k\vvec.
SM2. The distributive law over scalar addition: (k + \ell)\uvec = k\uvec + \ell \uvec.
SM3. The associative law for scalar multiplication: k(\ell \uvec) = (k\ell)\uvec.
SM4. The rule for multiplication by one: 1\uvec = \uvec.

subsection.p: The above definition is concerned about two operations: vector addition, denoted by \vvec + \wvec, and scalar multiplication, denoted by k\vvec or sometimes k \cdot \vvec. In the law of additive inverses, we have written -\uvec for (-1)\uvec.

Often, the scalars will be real numbers, but it is also possible to use scalars from a different field K. We also use the term K-vector space to refer to a vector space over a field K. When K = \mathbb{R}, we also speak of a real vector space, and when K = \mathbb{C}, we speak of a complex vector space. If the field is clear from the context, we often don’t mention it at all, and just speak of a “vector space”.
The elements of a vector space are called vectors. Our first example of a vector space is of course \mathbb{R}^n.

statement.p: The set \mathbb{R}^n of n-dimensional real column vectors, with the usual operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication, is a (real) vector space.
Proof: Properties (A1)–(A4) hold by our definition of vector addition, and properties (SM1)–(SM4) hold by our definition of vector-scalar multiplication.
The set \mathbb{C}^n of n-dimensional complex column vectors, with the usual operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication, is a (complex) vector space.

subsection.p: We now consider some other examples of vector spaces.

statement.p:
Let \mathbb{P}_2 be the set of all polynomials of degree at most 2 with real coefficients, i.e., expressions of the form p(x) = ax^2 + bx + c, where a,b,c \in \mathbb{R}. Define addition and scalar multiplication of polynomials in the usual way:
(ax^2 + bx + c) + (a'x^2 + b'x + c') = (a + a')x^2 + (b + b')x + (c + c')
k(ax^2 + bx + c) = kax^2 + kbx + kc.
Then \mathbb{P}_2 is a vector space.

Proof: To show that \mathbb{P}_2 is a vector space, we verify the 8 vector space axioms. Let p(x) = a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0, q(x) = b_2x^2 + b_1x + b_0, and r(x) = c_2x^2 + c_1x + c_0 be polynomials in \mathbb{P}_2 and let k,\ell be scalars.

(A1) We prove the commutative law of addition.
p(x) + q(x) \amp = (a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0) + (b_2x^2 + b_1x + b_0) \amp = (a_2 + b_2)x^2 + (a_1 + b_1)x + (a_0 + b_0).
Since addition in \mathbb{R} is commutative, this equals
(b_2 + a_2)x^2 + (b_1 + a_1)x + (b_0 + a_0) = q(x) + p(x).

(A2) We prove the associative law of addition.
(p(x) + q(x)) + r(x) \amp = ((a_2 + b_2)x^2 + (a_1 + b_1)x + (a_0 + b_0)) + (c_2x^2 + c_1x + c_0)
\amp = ((a_2 + b_2) + c_2)x^2 + ((a_1 + b_1) + c_1)x + ((a_0 + b_0) + c_0).
By associativity in \mathbb{R}, this equals
(a_2 + (b_2 + c_2))x^2 + (a_1 + (b_1 + c_1))x + (a_0 + (b_0 + c_0)) \amp = p(x) + (q(x) + r(x)).

(A3) To prove the existence of an additive unit, let 0(x) = 0x^2 + 0x + 0, the so‑called zero polynomial. Then
p(x) + 0(x) \amp = (a_2 + 0)x^2 + (a_1 + 0)x + (a_0 + 0) \amp = p(x).

(A4) We prove the law of additive inverses.
p(x) + (-p(x)) \amp = (a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0) + (-a_2x^2 - a_1x - a_0)
\amp = (a_2 - a_2)x^2 + (a_1 - a_1)x + (a_0 - a_0) \amp = 0(x).

(SM1) We prove the distributive law over vector addition.
k(p(x) + q(x)) \amp = k((a_2 + b_2)x^2 + (a_1 + b_1)x + (a_0 + b_0))
\amp = (ka_2 + kb_2)x^2 + (ka_1 + kb_1)x + (ka_0 + kb_0)
\amp = kp(x) + kq(x).

(SM2) We prove the distributive law over scalar addition.
(k + \ell)p(x) \amp = (k + \ell)(a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0)
\amp = ka_2x^2 + ka_1x + ka_0 + \ell a_2x^2 + \ell a_1x + \ell a_0
\amp = kp(x) + \ell p(x).

(SM3) We prove the associative law for scalar multiplication.
k(\ell p(x)) \amp = k(\ell a_2x^2 + \ell a_1x + \ell a_0) \amp = (k\ell)a_2x^2 + (k\ell)a_1x + (k\ell)a_0 \amp = (k\ell)p(x).

(SM4) Finally, we prove the rule for multiplication by one.
1p(x) \amp = 1(a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0) \amp = a_2x^2 + a_1x + a_0 \amp = p(x).
Since the operations of addition and scalar multiplication on \mathbb{P}_2 satisfy the 8 vector space axioms, \mathbb{P}_2 is a vector space.

Let M_{m,n}(\mathbb{R}) be the set of all m\times n-matrices with real entries, together with the usual operations of matrix addition and scalar multiplication. Then M_{m,n}(\mathbb{R}) is a vector space.
Proof: The properties (A1)–(A4) hold by our definition of matrix addition, and the properties (SM1)–(SM4) hold by our definition of scalar-matrix multiplication.

Let V denote the set of 2\times3-matrices. Let us define a non-standard addition in V by A\oplus B = A for all matrices A,B\in V. Let scalar multiplication in V be the usual scalar multiplication of matrices. We show that V is not a vector space.

In order to show that V is not a vector space, it suffices to find one of the 8 axioms that is not satisfied. We will begin by examining the axioms for addition until one is found which does not hold. In fact, for this example, the very first axiom fails.

Let
A=\begin{pmatrix}1\amp 0 \amp 0\\0\amp 0 \amp 0\end{pmatrix}, B=\begin{pmatrix}0\amp 0\amp 0\\1\amp 0\amp 0\end{pmatrix}.
Then A\oplus B=A and B\oplus A=B. Since A\neq B, we have A\oplus B\neq B\oplus A, so property (A1) is false.

Let X be a nonempty set and define \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}} to be the set of functions defined on X and valued in \mathbb{R}. In other words, the elements of \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}} are functions f:X\to \mathbb{R}. The sum of two functions is defined by (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x), and scalar multiplication is defined by (kf)(x)=k(f(x)). Then \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}} is a vector space.
To verify that \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}} is a vector space, we must prove the 8 axioms of vector spaces. Let f,g,h be functions in \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}}, and let k,\ell be scalars.
Recall that two functions f,g are equal if for all x\in X, we have f(x)=g(x).
(A1) For all x\in X, (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x)=g(x)+f(x)=(g+f)(x). Therefore f+g=g+f.
(A2) For all x\in X, ((f+g)+h)(x)=f(x)+(g(x)+h(x))=(f+(g+h))(x). Therefore (f+g)+h=f+(g+h).
(A3) Let 0 denote the function defined by 0(x)=0. Then (f+0)(x)=f(x) for all x, so f+0=f.
(A4) Let -f be defined by (-f)(x)=-f(x). Then (f+(-f))(x)=0 for all x, so f+(-f)=0.
(SM1) For all x, (k(f+g))(x)=k(f(x)+g(x))=(kf+kg)(x), hence k(f+g)=kf+kg.
(SM2) ((k+\ell)f)(x)=kf(x)+\ell f(x), so (k+\ell)f=kf+\ell f.
(SM3) ((k\ell)f)(x)=k(\ell f(x)), so (k\ell)f=k(\ell f).
(SM4) (1f)(x)=f(x) for all x, so 1f=f.
It follows that \mathrm{Func}_{X,\mathbb{R}} satisfies all the required axioms and is a vector space.

subsection.p: For the next two examples of vector spaces, we leave the proofs as an exercise.

statement.p: A sequence of real numbers, i.e. elements of \mathbb{R} is an infinite list (a_0,a_1,a_2,a_3,\ldots), where a_i\in \mathbb{R} for all i. We also use the notation (a_i)_{i\in\mathbb{N}}, or occasionally (a_i), to denote such a sequence. Let \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}} be the set of sequences of real numbers.

Let \mathbb{P} be the set of all polynomials (of any degree) with real coefficients, i.e., expressions of the form
p(x)=a_nx^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1x+a_0 , where n\ge 0 and a_0,\ldots,a_n\in \mathbb{R}. Addition and scalar multiplication of polynomials are defined in the usual way. Then \mathbb{P} is a vector space.

li.p: Explain why \mathbb{P}_{n} is closed under the operation of usual polynomial addition. That is, explain why {\pvec}+ \qvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n} if \pvec, \qvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n}.
Explain why \mathbb{P}_{n} is closed under scalar multiplication. That is, explain why c{\pvec}\in \mathbb{P}_{n} if c \in \mathbb{R} and \pvec\in \mathbb{P}_{n}.
Identify/describe the zero element {\zerovec}\in \mathbb{P}_{n} in axiom (A3). Then show that \pvec +{\zerovec}= \pvec for every \pvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n}.
For an element \pvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n}, identify/describe the additive inverse -\pvec in axiom A4.
For elements \pvec, \qvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n}, show that axiom (SM1) holds.
If \pvec, \qvec \in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then both \pvec and \qvec are polynomials of degree at most n. Adding two such polynomials yields a polynomial \pvec + \qvec having degree at most n as well.

If \pvec\in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then \pvec(x)=a_{0}+a_{1}x+a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n}. For any constant c, we then have (c\pvec)(x) = c(a_{0}+a_{1}x+a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n}) = c\cdot a_{0} + (c\cdot a_{1})x + (c \cdot a_{2})x^{2} + \hdots + (c \cdot a_{n})x^{n} which is clearly also in \mathbb{P}_{n}.

{\zerovec}(x) = 0 + 0x + 0x^{2} + \hdots 0x^{n}. If \pvec is any element in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then \pvec(x) = a_{0} + a_{1}x + a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n} so that \pvec(x) +{\zerovec}(x) = (a_{0} + a_{1}x + a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n}) + (0 + 0x + 0x^{2} + \hdots 0x^{n}) = (a_{0}+0) + (a_{1}+0)x + (a_{2}+0)x^{2} + \hdots + (a_{n}+0)x^{n} = a_{0} + a_{1}x + a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n} = \pvec(x).

If \pvec is any element in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then \pvec(x) = a_{0} + a_{1}x + a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n} and the additive inverse is -\pvec defined by -\pvec(x) = -a_{0} -a_{1}x - a_{2}x^{2} - \hdots - a_{n}x^{n}.

If \pvec is any element in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then \pvec(x) = a_{0} + a_{1}x + a_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + a_{n}x^{n}. Similarly, if \qvec is any element in \mathbb{P}_{n}, then \qvec(x) = b_{0} + b_{1}x + b_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + b_{n}x^{n}. For any constant k \in \mathbb{R}, we then have (k(\pvec+\qvec))(x) = k(\pvec(x) + \qvec(x)) = k((a_{0}+b_{0}) + (a_{1}+b_{1})x + (a_{2}+b_{2})x^{2} + \hdots + (a_{n}+b_{n})x^{n} = ka_{0} + kb_{0} + ka_{1}x + kb_{1}x + ka_{2}x^{2} + kb_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + ka_{n}x^{n} + kb_{n}x^{n} = (ka_{0} + ka_{1}x + ka_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + ka_{n}x^{n}) + (kb_{0} + kb_{1}x + kb_{2}x^{2} + \hdots + kb_{n}x^{n}) = (k\pvec)(x) + (k\qvec)(x).

statement.p:
Let (a,b) be an open interval of real numbers. Define C(a,b) to be the set of continuous, real-valued functions \fvec defined on (a,b). That is, elements are continuous functions \fvec: (a,b) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}. Define the addition of continous functions by
(\fvec+\gvec)(x) = \fvec(x)+\gvec(x)
and scalar multiplication by
(c\fvec)(x) = c(\fvec(x)).
We investigate whether C(a,b) is a vector space or not.

ol.li: Explain why C(a,b) is closed under addition as defined. That is, explain why {\fvec}+ \gvec \in C(a,b) if \fvec, \gvec \in C(a,b).
Explain why C(a,b) is closed under scalar multiplication. That is, explain why c{\fvec}\in C(a,b) if c \in \mathbb{R} and \fvec \in C(a,b).
Identify/describe the zero element 0 and additive inverse -\fvec of elements \fvec \in C(a,b). How do you know that these elements are also in C(a,b)?
Carefully show that axiom (SM2) holds for C(a,b).
Is C(a,b) a vector space?
Adding two continuous functions gives another continuous function.

Think of multiplying by c as vertically stretching the graph of a function f(x). If c < 0, then it is also reflected about the x-axis. In any case, if a function f(x) is continuous, then vertically stretching and/or reflecting about the x-axis will not change the continuous nature of the graph.

The {\bfseries{0}} element in C(a,b) is the zero function {\bfseries{0}}(x) = 0 for every x. Since constant functions are continuous, the zero function is continuous. If \fvec is continuous on (a,b), then -\fvec is the function having graph reflected about the x-axis and is therefore also continuous.
If \uvec \in C(a,b), then \uvec(x) is a continuous function on (a,b). For any k, l \in \mathbb{R}, we then have ((k+l)\uvec)(x) = k\uvec(x) + l\uvec(x).
yes.

statement.p: Let \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}} denote the space of all infinite sequences a_{0}, a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots of real numbers. We denote such a sequence as (\avec_{n}) where it is understood that the subscripts n run from 0 to \infty. Addition is defined by (\avec_{n}) + (\bvec_{n}) = (\avec_{n}+\bvec_{n}) and scalar multiplication by c(\avec_{n}) = (c\avec_{n}) for c \in \mathbb{R}.

ol.li: Identify the zero element {\zerovec} in \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}} from axiom (A3).
Identify the additive inverse of (\avec_{n}) \in \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}} and then show that (\avec_{n}) + (-\avec_{n}) ={\zerovec} for every (\avec_{n}) \in \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}.
Is \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}} a vector space?
Let c denote the space of all convergent sequences of real numbers under the same operations. Is c a vector space or not?
Let c_{1} denote the space of all real sequences that converge to 1 under the same operations. Is c_{1} a vector space or not?
Let c_{0} denote the space of all real sequences that converge to 0 under the same operations. Is c_{0} a vector space or not?
Let c^{+} denote the space of all real positive sequences under the same operations. Is c^{+} a vector space or not?
The zero element {\zerovec} is the sequence 0,0,0,0,\hdots

For (\avec_{n}) \in \mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N}}, we have (\avec_{n}) = a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots. The additive inverse (-\avec_{n}) is the sequence -a_{1}, -a_{2}, -a_{3}, \ldots. Then, (\avec_{n}) + (-\avec_{n}) = (a_{1} , a_{2} , a_{3} , \ldots) + (-a_{1}, - a_{2}, - a_{3}, \ldots) =( (a_{1}-a_{1}) , (a_{2}-a_{2}) , (a_{3}-a_{3}), \ldots) = (0,0,0,\ldots)={\zerovec}.
Yes.
Yes; note that the sum of two convergent sequences is also convergent. So too is a scalar multiple of a convergent sequence.
No. This is easy to see by taking the constant sequence 1,1,1,1,\ldots and adding it to itself. This sequence does not converge to 1.
Yes. The sum of two such sequences also converges to 0 and any constant multiple of a sequence converging to 0 also converges to 0.
No. There is no additive unit - see axiom (A3).

statement.p: In any vector space, the following are true:

ol.li: The additive unit is unique. In other words, whenever \uvec+\vvec=\uvec, then \vvec=\zerovec.
Additive inverses are unique. In other words, whenever \uvec+\vvec=\zerovec, then \vvec=-\uvec.
0\uvec=\zerovec for all vectors \uvec.
The following cancellation law holds: if \uvec+\wvec=\vvec+\wvec, then \uvec=\vvec.

proof.p: We prove the first three properties, and leave the last one as an exercise. Assume V is any vector space over a field K.

(a) Consider arbitrary vectors \uvec,\vvec\in V and assume \uvec+\vvec=\uvec. Applying the law (A1) (commutative law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec+\uvec=\uvec. Adding -\uvec to both sides of the equation, we have (\vvec+\uvec)+(-\uvec)=\uvec+(-\uvec). Applying the law (A2) (associative law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec+(\uvec+(-\uvec))=\uvec+(-\uvec). Applying the law (A4) (additive inverse law) to both sides of the equation, we have \vvec+\zerovec=\zerovec. Applying the law (A3) (additive unit law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec=\zerovec. This proves that whenever \uvec+\vvec=\uvec, then \vvec=\zerovec, or in other words, \vvec=\zerovec is the only element acting as an additive unit.

(b) Consider arbitrary vectors \uvec,\vvec\in V and assume \uvec+\vvec=\zerovec. Applying the law (A1) (commutative law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec+\uvec=\zerovec. Adding -\uvec to both sides of the equation, we have (\vvec+\uvec)+(-\uvec)=\zerovec+(-\uvec). Applying the law (A2) (associative law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec+(\uvec+(-\uvec))=\zerovec+(-\uvec). Applying the law (A4) (additive inverse law) to the left-hand side, we have \vvec+\zerovec=\zerovec+(-\uvec). Applying the law (A1) (commutative law) to the right-hand side, we have \vvec+\zerovec=-\uvec+\zerovec. Applying the law (A3) (additive unit law) to both sides of the equation, we have \vvec=-\uvec. This proves that whenever \uvec+\vvec=\zerovec, then \vvec=-\uvec, or in other words, \vvec=-\uvec is the only element acting as an additive inverse of \uvec.

(c) First, note that the scalar 0\in K satisfies the property 0+0=0, by property (A3) of the definition of a field. Now let \uvec\in V be any vector. Using the vector space law (SM2) (distributive law over scalar addition) and 0+0=0, we have 0\uvec+0\uvec=(0+0)\uvec=0\uvec. Next, we use a small trick: add -(0\uvec) to both sides of the equation. This gives (0\uvec+0\uvec)+(-(0\uvec))=0\uvec+(-(0\uvec)). Applying the additional laws (A2), (A4), and (A3), we have 0\uvec+(0\uvec+(-(0\uvec)))=0\uvec+(-(0\uvec)), 0\uvec+\zerovec=\zerovec, and 0\uvec=\zerovec. This proves that 0\uvec=\zerovec holds for all vectors \uvec, as desired.
(d) This is left as an exercise.

section.subsection:
Summary
The main purpose of this section was to investigate the definition of a vector space over a field through identification of the required operations and axioms.
Standard examples of vector spaces include \mathbb{R}^n, polynomial spaces, matrix spaces, and spaces of functions.
We can determine whether a given set with specified operations is a vector space by checking the vector space axioms. We can also identify examples of sets that fail to be vector spaces and explain which axioms are violated.
The vector space axioms derive basic consequences such as the uniqueness of the zero vector and additive inverses.
``
